title: The Early Years
content:
The gravel crunched beneath the tires of our old station wagon, a sound that always signaled the transition from the hum of the city to the silence of the country.

Dad was humming along to a tune on the radio, his fingers tapping rhythmically on the steering wheel. Mom was asleep in the passenger seat, her head lolling against the window, catching the afternoon sun.

I sat in the back, wedged between a cooler full of sandwiches and a stack of comic books, watching the landscape shift. The tall, rigid buildings of the suburbs gave way to rolling green hills and fences that seemed to stretch on forever. The air coming through the cracked window smelled different here—richer, heavier with the scent of pine and damp earth.

DRAFTING IN PROGRESS

It was a three-hour drive, but to a seven-year-old, it felt like an eternity. We passed the familiar landmarks: the rusted red barn with the faded 'Chew Mail Pouch Tobacco' sign, the bridge over the creek that always looked like it was about to collapse, and finally, the narrow gravel road that wound through the pines to Grandma's house.

chat.agentSubtitle: AI Writes is helping you bridge knowledge gaps

messages.text: Let's work on Chapter 3. I see you want to focus on your childhood in the countryside. Shall we start with the description of the old farmhouse? I can help outline the sensory details of the kitchen in the morning.
I think I'm writing for women in their mid-40s who feel stuck in their careers but feel it's too late to pivot. They feel successful on paper but empty inside.
